title: Sound waves and hearing
examTip: Sound cannot travel through a vacuum — there are no particles to vibrate. This is the key distinction between sound and electromagnetic waves. Always state this clearly in exam answers.
introTips: Sound is a longitudinal wave — particles in the medium oscillate parallel to the direction the wave travels.
Sound needs a medium (particles) to travel — it cannot travel through a vacuum.
Speed of sound varies with medium: fastest in solids, slower in liquids, slowest in gases.
Frequency determines pitch; amplitude determines loudness.
Ultrasound (above 20,000 Hz) is used medically because it's safe and reflects off internal boundaries.
intro: You can hear a thunderclap from kilometres away, a doctor can scan your unborn sibling using sound waves, and ships map the ocean floor using bouncing sound pulses. Sound is one of the most fundamental ways we interact with the world — and it has fascinating physics behind it. Unlike the electromagnetic waves that carry your Wi-Fi signal, sound needs something physical to travel through: particles bumping into each other. That single fact — that sound requires a medium — explains why space is silent, why sound travels faster through steel than through air, and how engineers design concert halls for perfect acoustics.

keyPoints.heading: Sound as a longitudinal wave
keyPoints.content: Sound is a mechanical, longitudinal wave. It is produced by vibrating objects (a speaker cone, vocal cords, a drum skin) which alternately push and pull the particles in the surrounding medium. This creates compressions (regions of high pressure where particles are pushed together) and rarefactions (regions of low pressure where particles are spread apart) that travel outward from the source. Because the particle oscillation is parallel to the direction of travel, sound is longitudinal. Because it requires particles to vibrate, sound cannot travel through a vacuum — there are no particles to carry the wave. This is why there is no sound in space, and why you cannot hear an explosion on the Moon.
writeBullets: Sound is a mechanical longitudinal wave — particles oscillate parallel to direction of travel
Produced by vibrating objects — speaker cone, vocal cords, drum skin
Creates compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure) in the medium
Cannot travel through a vacuum — requires a medium (particles) to carry the wave
No sound in space — no particles to transmit the vibrations

keyTerms.def: A wave where particle oscillation is parallel to the direction of travel; sound is the key example
A region of high pressure in a sound wave where particles are pushed together
A region of low pressure in a sound wave where particles are spread apart
A wave that requires a physical medium (particles) to travel — cannot travel through a vacuum

keyPoints.memTip: Sound = SLAM: Sound is a Longitudinal, hAs Medium. Cannot travel through vacuum.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students frequently say sound 'travels through air molecules' — redirect to: sound is transmitted by the vibration of air molecules; the molecules themselves don't travel along with the wave.

keyPoints.heading: Speed of sound in different media
keyPoints.content: The speed of sound depends on the medium it travels through, not on the frequency or amplitude. The key rule: sound travels fastest through solids, slower through liquids, and slowest through gases. This is because in solids, particles are closest together and most strongly bonded — they transmit vibrations very efficiently. In gases, particles are far apart and interact weakly, so vibrations spread more slowly. Approximate speeds: steel ~5000 m/s, water ~1500 m/s, air ~340 m/s. The speed of sound in air is approximately 340 m/s at room temperature (compare this to the speed of light, 3×10⁸ m/s — which is why you see lightning before you hear thunder). Sound can be reflected (echoes), refracted, and diffracted, just like other waves.
writeBullets: Speed of sound: fastest in solids, slower in liquids, slowest in gases
Solids — particles closest together → vibrations transmitted most efficiently
Speed in air ≈ 340 m/s; in water ≈ 1500 m/s; in steel ≈ 5000 m/s
Speed depends on medium, not on frequency or amplitude
Light travels much faster than sound — explains seeing lightning before hearing thunder

keyTerms.def: The rate at which sound travels through a medium; ~340 m/s in air at room temperature; faster in denser media

keyPoints.memTip: Solid > Liquid > Gas for sound speed. Particles closer together = faster transfer of vibrations. Air ≈ 340 m/s.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse 'denser medium = faster sound' with light (where denser media slow it down) — emphasise the opposite relationship and why (particle spacing and bonding).

keyPoints.heading: Frequency, amplitude, pitch, and loudness
keyPoints.content: Two key properties of a sound wave determine how we perceive it: Frequency → pitch: higher frequency = higher pitch. A piccolo produces high-frequency sound; a bass guitar produces low-frequency sound. Frequency is measured in hertz (Hz). Amplitude → loudness: larger amplitude = louder sound. A loud shout has greater amplitude than a whisper. Measured indirectly in decibels (dB). The human hearing range is approximately 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Below 20 Hz: infrasound (too low to hear — produced by earthquakes and whales). Above 20,000 Hz: ultrasound (too high to hear — used by bats, dolphins, and in medicine/technology). These limits vary with age — older adults often cannot hear above 15,000–17,000 Hz.
writeBullets: Frequency → pitch: higher frequency = higher pitch
Amplitude → loudness: larger amplitude = louder sound
Human hearing range: 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz
Infrasound — below 20 Hz; not audible to humans (earthquakes, whales)
Ultrasound — above 20,000 Hz; not audible to humans; used in medicine and sonar

keyTerms.def: The perceived highness or lowness of a sound; determined by frequency — higher frequency = higher pitch
The perceived volume of a sound; determined by amplitude — larger amplitude = louder
Sound below 20 Hz — below the human hearing threshold; produced by earthquakes and large animals
Sound above 20,000 Hz — above the human hearing threshold; used in medical imaging and sonar

keyPoints.memTip: FrEQ = pitch (Frequency → pitch). AMP = volume (AMPlitude → loudness). Human range: 20 to 20,000 Hz.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes reverse frequency and pitch or amplitude and loudness — use clear cause-effect language: 'increasing frequency increases pitch'.

keyPoints.heading: Echoes, ultrasound, and sonar
keyPoints.content: An echo is the reflection of a sound wave off a hard surface. You can use echo timing to calculate distances: distance = speed × time, but remember the sound travels there and back — so distance to the reflector = (speed × time) ÷ 2. Example: an echo returns after 0.4 s and speed of sound = 340 m/s → total distance = 340 × 0.4 = 136 m → distance to wall = 136 ÷ 2 = 68 m. Ultrasound (above 20,000 Hz) has important uses: medical imaging — ultrasound pulses reflect off boundaries between tissues (e.g. between a baby and surrounding fluid), and the reflected pulses are used to build up an image; sonar — ships send ultrasound pulses toward the seabed and time the returning echo to measure water depth; industrial cleaning — high-frequency vibrations dislodge dirt. Ultrasound is preferred over X-rays for pregnancy scanning because it does not ionise tissue — it is safe for the baby.
writeBullets: Echo — reflection of sound from a hard surface
Distance = speed × time; but sound travels there AND back — divide by 2
Ultrasound (>20,000 Hz) — medical imaging (pregnancy scans), sonar, industrial cleaning
Ultrasound safer than X-rays — non-ionising; preferred for pregnancy scanning
Sonar — measures water depth or detects objects using timed ultrasound pulses

keyTerms.def: The reflection of a sound wave from a hard surface; used to calculate distances
Sound navigation and ranging — uses timed ultrasound pulses to measure distances underwater
Using reflected ultrasound pulses to build an image of internal structures (e.g. a foetus in the womb)

keyPoints.memTip: Echo distance = (speed × time) ÷ 2 — divide by 2 because sound makes the return trip too. Ultrasound = safe scanning (no ionising radiation).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Echo calculation questions almost always catch students who forget to halve the time — stress that the wave travels to the reflector AND back, so you halve the total distance or total time.

0.question: Why can sound not travel through a vacuum (such as outer space)?
options: Sound is a mechanical wave that requires particles to vibrate — there are no particles in a vacuum
Sound travels too slowly to cross large distances
Sound waves are transverse and cannot pass through empty space
The frequency of sound is too low to travel without air resistance
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Sound needs a medium — particles to pass vibrations along. A vacuum has no particles, so there is nothing to carry the wave.
0.wrongFeedback: Sound requires a medium (particles) to travel — it is a mechanical wave. Electromagnetic waves (like light) can travel through a vacuum; sound cannot because there are no particles to vibrate.

1.question: A sound wave has its frequency increased while its amplitude stays the same. How does this change the sound you hear?
options: The pitch increases but the loudness stays the same
The pitch and loudness both increase
The loudness increases but the pitch stays the same
The sound becomes quieter and higher
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Frequency controls pitch — higher frequency = higher pitch. Amplitude controls loudness — since amplitude is unchanged, loudness stays the same.
1.wrongFeedback: Remember: frequency → pitch, amplitude → loudness. Changing frequency changes only pitch; amplitude is unchanged, so loudness stays the same.

3.question: A ship sends an ultrasound pulse downward and receives the echo 0.6 seconds later. The speed of sound in water is 1500 m/s. How deep is the water?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Total distance = 1500 × 0.6 = 900 m. Divide by 2 (sound travels to the bottom and back): 900 ÷ 2 = 450 m.
3.wrongFeedback: Use: total distance = speed × time = 1500 × 0.6 = 900 m. Then halve it (sound travels there AND back): 900 ÷ 2 = 450 m depth.

commonMistakes: Saying sound travels through a vacuum — sound is a mechanical wave that requires particles to transmit vibrations; it cannot travel through empty space.
Forgetting to divide by 2 in echo/sonar calculations — the sound travels to the reflector and back, so you must halve the total distance (or total time).
Confusing frequency with amplitude — frequency determines pitch (not loudness); amplitude determines loudness (not pitch).
Saying sound travels fastest in gases because 'there's more room' — it is the opposite: sound travels fastest in solids because particles are closest together and bonded more strongly.
Describing ultrasound as a type of electromagnetic wave — ultrasound is a sound wave (mechanical, longitudinal); it is not electromagnetic.
examTips: If asked why sound cannot travel through a vacuum, always link to the particle model — no particles means no medium for vibrations to pass through.
In echo/sonar distance calculations, always write: distance to object = (speed × time) ÷ 2. Never forget the ÷ 2.
When comparing ultrasound and X-rays for medical imaging, state that ultrasound is non-ionising — this is the key safety advantage.
For frequency/pitch and amplitude/loudness questions, use the exact exam language: 'higher frequency produces a higher pitch'; 'greater amplitude produces a louder sound'.
Speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s — this value may be given in the question or you may need to recall it; check the question carefully.
revisionCardBullets: Sound — mechanical longitudinal wave; particle oscillation parallel to direction of travel
Cannot travel through a vacuum — requires a medium (particles) to propagate
Compressions — high pressure regions; rarefactions — low pressure regions
Speed of sound: solids fastest (~5000 m/s steel), liquids intermediate (~1500 m/s water), gases slowest (~340 m/s air)
Frequency → pitch: higher frequency = higher pitch
Amplitude → loudness: greater amplitude = louder sound
Infrasound: below 20 Hz (earthquakes, whales); Ultrasound: above 20,000 Hz (medicine, sonar)
Ultrasound medical imaging — reflects off tissue boundaries; non-ionising (safe for pregnancy)
Sonar — measures water depth using timed ultrasound pulses
